component_identification.content: Skull components are minimal: each player receives a personal set of 4 discs/cards (3 flowers/roses and 1 skull), with identical backs and player-distinct fronts/sets. Many editions support up to 6 players, so full box contains 24 discs total. No board, no scoring pad, and no resource tokens are required under standard rules. Verify each player set has exactly one skull; missing a skull invalidates bluff balance immediately.

core_game_loop.content: Players begin by placing one disc face down. On turn, a player either adds one more disc to their own stack or starts/increases a bid on how many discs they can reveal without exposing a skull. Bidding continues until all but one bidder pass. The winning bidder reveals discs: their own stack first, then chosen top discs from opponents until reaching bid target. Success scores one point; hitting any skull fails and costs a disc. First to two successful challenges wins.

detailed_rules.content: Each player begins round with all remaining discs and starts a face-down stack in front of themselves. Play proceeds clockwise. If no bid is active, your legal choices are: add one disc to your stack or open bidding with a number not exceeding currently available top discs. Once bidding starts, subsequent players must either raise the bid or pass; no further disc placement occurs. When one bidder remains, challenge resolution begins. Challenger must reveal from their own stack first, top to bottom. If no skull appears, they continue revealing top disc from any players’ stacks one at a time until they either satisfy bid count (success) or reveal a skull (failure). Revealing a skull ends challenge immediately. On failure, challenger loses one random disc (or according to edition procedure) from their remaining set; concealed identity of removed disc preserves uncertainty where applicable. On success, challenger gains one point/flower token toward victory. Round then resets with all unrevealed discs returned to owners’ supplies and new opening placements. Eliminated players (no discs) are out. Bluff texture comes from memory of prior reveals and stack-growth behavior. Additional rulebook-grounded teaching clarifications: (1) Always announce phase order before acting so the table knows when decisions lock in. (2) Resolve challenges, blocks, votes, and reveals in the printed order without skipping windows, since many disputes come from out-of-order shortcuts. (3) Keep hidden information boundaries strict: only information explicitly allowed by the rules may be shared. (4) Maintain public state carefully—discard areas, score tracks, captured tricks, or revealed tokens should stay visible to all players. (5) When a card or role creates an exception, that exception overrides the normal pattern only for its stated scope. (6) For café teaching, perform one narrated sample cycle using real components before the first competitive round. (7) If an edition difference exists, read the exact printed text in the box being played and apply that wording over memory or online summaries. (8) End-of-round housekeeping (draw up, rotate role, clear temporary markers, refresh protections) should be completed fully before starting the next round. (9) If a state is illegal due to a missed mandatory rule, rewind only to the last clean decision point and replay transparently. (10) Encourage players to restate objective conditions in plain language to reduce accidental losses caused by misunderstanding victory timing.

scoring_and_endgame.content: Scoring is binary per challenge: successful bidder gains one point; failed bidder gains none and loses a disc. Standard win condition is first player to score two successful challenges. Alternate terminal condition occurs when only one player remains with discs due to eliminations; that survivor wins if your edition includes survival resolution. There are no additional bonus points.

beginner_strategy.content: Early rounds, prioritize information gathering over hero bids—watch who repeatedly opens with conservative placement. Remember that challengers must flip their own stack first; this makes self-stack composition critical to bid credibility. Mix truthful and deceptive bid behavior so opponents cannot infer whether your top is skull or flower. When an opponent is one point from victory, pressure with raises that force them into high-risk reveals. Keep a mental log of likely skull locations from previous failed challenges. Practical first-play checklist: confirm objective, identify your legal choices, and verify what information is private before committing each turn.

metadata.notes: Rewrite sprint using official rulebook text from skull.txt as primary source. Reference snippet: CONTENTS: 6 tribes. Each tribe has: 3 flower-discs, 1 skull-disc, 1 card-playing mat. 3-6 players.
